sentiment.rationale: Commands the elders to receive the law with blessings and cursings; Edward Partridge called as bishop with warning about discipleship.
The 'Law' section with moral commandments, consecration requirements, church discipline, and healing instructions—predominantly instructive and corrective.
Dramatic Second Coming prophecy with thunders, earthquakes, and trumpet blasts calling nations to repent before the Millennium.
Brief command to gather the elders, promising the Spirit and instructing them to care for the poor—calm and organizational.
Extended Olivet discourse with destruction of Jerusalem, Second Coming signs, and judgments, balanced by the New Jerusalem as a city of peace and refuge.
Teaching on spiritual gifts given for the benefit of all—knowing Jesus is the Son of God, healing, prophecy, tongues, discernment—warm and edifying.
Brief, peaceful assignment of John Whitmer as church historian and record keeper.
Practical instructions to share lands with eastern converts and save money for future Zion—calm, organizational.
Corrects Shaker heresies about celibacy, diet, and Christ's return; affirms marriage and stewardship, with Second Coming urgency.
Teaches discernment of spirits with sobering warnings, but concludes tenderly: 'Fear not, little children, for you are mine.'
Practical organization of consecration—equal portions, storehouse, property deeds—peaceful and administrative in tone.
Missionary companionship assignments to Missouri with a pattern for recognizing the Spirit through contrition and meekness.
Brief, encouraging call to Sidney Gilbert as elder and purchasing agent—affirming and forward-looking.
Addresses broken covenants in Thompson with woe upon offenders, commanding the faithful to flee to Missouri.
Encouraging call to W. W. Phelps for baptism, ordination, and printing—brief and affirming.
Sharp rebukes and woes upon both rich who hoard and poor who covet, with revised missionary assignments—strongly corrective.
Designates Independence as Zion's center place and temple site—hopeful and organizational with land-purchasing instructions.
Promises blessings after tribulation, teaches agency and being anxiously engaged in good causes, with instructions for building Zion.
Beautiful Sabbath-keeping revelation promising the fulness of the earth—food, raiment, gardens, vineyards—to gladden the heart and enliven the soul.
Rebukes elders who hide their talents out of fear of man, then gives practical travel instructions for the return from Missouri.

summaries.summary: The Lord commands the elders to assemble and receive His law, calls Edward Partridge as the first bishop of the church, and compares him to Nathanael.
The Lord gives the comprehensive law of the church including moral commandments, the law of consecration, instructions on healing, church discipline, and the New Jerusalem.
The Lord establishes that only Joseph Smith receives revelations for the church, then prophesies the Second Coming with thunders, earthquakes, and the Millennium.
The Lord commands the elders to gather from all directions for a conference, promising to pour out His Spirit and instructing them to care for the poor.
Christ recounts His Olivet prophecy of Jerusalem's destruction and Second Coming signs, then reveals the New Jerusalem as a land of peace and refuge for the saints.
The Lord teaches about the gifts of the Spirit—knowledge, faith, healing, miracles, prophecy, tongues, and discernment—given for the benefit of all who love Him.
The Lord appoints John Whitmer to keep the church record and history continually, relieving Oliver Cowdery for other duties.
The Lord instructs the Ohio saints to share lands with eastern converts, save money, and prepare to purchase land for the future city of Zion.
The Lord sends missionaries to the Shakers, correcting false doctrines about celibacy, diet, and Christ's return, and affirming that marriage is ordained of God.
The Lord teaches elders to discern true and false spirits, declaring that which edifies is of God and that light grows brighter unto the perfect day.
The Lord instructs Bishop Partridge on organizing consecration—equal portions, property deeds, a storehouse—so the saints may be one and deal honestly.
The Lord assigns missionary companionships for the journey to Missouri and gives a pattern for recognizing the Spirit through contrition, meekness, and edification.
The Lord calls Sidney Gilbert to be ordained an elder, serve as the church's purchasing agent, and journey with Joseph Smith to Missouri.
The Lord addresses broken covenants among the Thompson saints, commanding them to flee to Missouri and pronouncing woe upon the covenant-breakers.
The Lord calls William W. Phelps to be baptized, ordained an elder, and assist Oliver Cowdery with the church's printing and school materials.
The Lord revokes and reassigns missionary callings, rebukes Ezra Thayre for pride, and pronounces woes upon both the greedy rich and the covetous poor.
The Lord designates Independence, Missouri as the center place of Zion, identifies the temple site, and assigns roles for land purchasing and printing.
The Lord promises blessings after tribulation, teaches that men should be anxiously engaged in good causes of their own free will, and gives instructions for Zion.
The Lord commands Sabbath observance with a broken heart and contrite spirit, promising the faithful the fulness of the earth for food, raiment, and joy.
The Lord rebukes elders who hide their talents out of fear, then gives travel instructions for the return journey from Missouri through St. Louis.

crossReferences.note: Both teach that looking upon a woman with lust is a sin of the heart equivalent to adultery.
'Inasmuch as ye impart... ye will do it unto me' echoes Christ's teaching about serving the least of these.
Both promise progressive revelation—knowledge upon knowledge, light growing brighter unto the perfect day.
Christ recounts and expands His Olivet Discourse from Matthew 24 with additional Restoration details.
The Jews asking 'What are these wounds?' fulfills Zechariah's prophecy about wounds in the house of friends.
The parable of the ten virgins applied—the wise who took the Holy Spirit for their guide shall abide the day.
Paul's teaching on gifts of the Spirit closely parallels this D&C enumeration of spiritual gifts.
Affirms marriage as ordained of God and that 'they twain shall be one flesh,' correcting Shaker celibacy.
Both describe the path of the righteous as a light that grows brighter and brighter unto the perfect day.
Both teach that men should be actively engaged in good works and not require compulsion in all things.
Christ restates the two great commandments—love God with all your heart, and love your neighbor as yourself.
Both identify a broken heart and contrite spirit as the sacrifice acceptable to God.

verses.reason: 'He that receiveth my law and doeth it, the same is my disciple'—clear definition of true discipleship.
Edward Partridge compared to 'Nathanael of old, in whom there is no guile'—rare individual commendation.
'Thou shalt love thy wife with all thy heart, and shalt cleave unto her and none else'—foundational marriage commandment.
'Thou shalt ask, and thou shalt receive revelation upon revelation, knowledge upon knowledge'—progressive revelation promise.
'Thou wilt remember the poor, and consecrate of thy properties for their support'—the law of consecration.
'Let the solemnities of eternity rest upon your minds'—a unique and powerful phrase frequently quoted.
The Lord's lament listing every way He has called upon the nations—voice, thunders, earthquakes, pestilence, mercy.
'They that are wise and have received the truth, and have taken the Holy Spirit for their guide, and have not been deceived'—the wise virgins defined.
'These wounds are the wounds with which I was wounded in the house of my friends'—Christ's poignant revelation to the Jews at His return.
'And it shall be called the New Jerusalem, a land of peace, a city of refuge, a place of safety for the saints.'
'To some it is given by the Holy Ghost to know that Jesus Christ is the Son of God'—the first and primary spiritual gift.
'To others it is given to believe on their words'—validating those who rely on the testimony of others.
'For all have not every gift given unto them; for there are many gifts, and to every man is given a gift by the Spirit of God.'
'Whoso forbiddeth to marry is not ordained of God, for marriage is ordained of God unto man'—clear declaration on the divine nature of marriage.
'Before the great day of the Lord shall come, Jacob shall flourish in the wilderness, and the Lamanites shall blossom as the rose.'
'That which is of God is light; and he that receiveth light, and continueth in God, receiveth more light; and that light groweth brighter and brighter until the perfect day.'
'That which doth not edify is not of God, and is darkness'—a simple, powerful test for discerning truth.
'Fear not, little children, for you are mine, and I have overcome the world'—tender reassurance from Christ.
'Whoso is found a faithful, a just, and a wise steward shall enter into the joy of his Lord, and shall inherit eternal life.'
'He that prayeth, whose spirit is contrite, the same is accepted of me if he obey mine ordinances'—a pattern for spiritual authenticity.
'He only is saved who endureth unto the end'—concise restatement of a fundamental gospel principle.
'Be patient in tribulation until I come; and, behold, I come quickly, and my reward is with me.'
Powerful woe to the rich: 'your riches will canker your souls... The harvest is past, the summer is ended, and my soul is not saved!'
'Blessed are the poor who are pure in heart, whose hearts are broken, and whose spirits are contrite, for they shall see the kingdom of God.'
'This is the land of promise, and the place for the city of Zion'—the revelation designating Independence, Missouri.
'The place which is now called Independence is the center place; and a spot for the temple is lying westward.'
'Men should be anxiously engaged in a good cause, and do many things of their own free will, and bring to pass much righteousness'—one of the most quoted D&C verses on agency.
'It is not meet that I should command in all things; for he that is compelled in all things, the same is a slothful and not a wise servant.'
'He who has repented of his sins, the same is forgiven, and I, the Lord, remember them no more.'
'Thou shalt offer a sacrifice unto the Lord thy God in righteousness, even that of a broken heart and a contrite spirit.'
'Thou shalt go to the house of prayer and offer up thy sacraments upon my holy day'—the Sabbath day commandment.
'For food and for raiment, for taste and for smell, to strengthen the body and to enliven the soul'—celebrates earth's bounty.
'They hide the talent which I have given unto them, because of the fear of man. Wo unto such'—stern warning against suppressing one's testimony.'
